perturbation.original_text: Diplomat Ambassador Eyewear Group agrees to comply with the laws and regulations of any state or territory in which they manufacture or have sub-contracted any Kathy Ireland product, especially pertaining to labor and safety issues.
perturbation.changed_text: Diplomat Ambassador Eyewear Group will endeavor to comply with applicable laws and regulations of any state or territory in which they manufacture or have sub-contracted any Kathy Ireland product, pertaining to labor and safety issues as they understand them.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'agrees to comply' to 'will endeavor to comply' and adding 'as they understand them', the obligation becomes vague and subjective. The company's understanding of the laws and regulations may not align with the actual legal requirements. This creates a loophole where non-compliance can be excused based on a subjective interpretation of the law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FLSA sets specific minimum wage and overtime pay standards. The modified text's ambiguity about compliance, especially 'as they understand them', directly contradicts the FLSA's requirement for strict adherence to minimum wage and overtime rules, regardless of the employer's subjective understanding.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: in the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA). The federal minimum wage is $7.25 per hour effective July 24, 2009. Many states also have minimum wage laws. Some state laws provide greater employee protections; employers must comply with both. The FLSA does not provide wage payment collection procedures for an employee’s usual or promised wages or commissions in excess of those required by the FLSA. However, some states do have laws under which such claims (sometimes including fringe benefits) may be filed. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 29 - LABOR 29 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 29 - LABOR CHAPTER 8 - FAIR LABOR STANDARDS Sec. 206 - Minimum wage From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §206. Minimum wage (a) Employees engaged in commerce; home workers in Puerto Rico and Virgin Islands; employees in American Samoa; seamen on American vessels; agricultural employees Every employer shall pay to each of his employees who in any workweek is engaged in commerce or in the production of goods for commerce, or is employed in an enterprise engaged in commerce or in the production of goods for commerce, wages at the following rates: (1) except as otherwise provided in this section, not less than— (A) $5.85 an hour, beginning on the 60th day after May 25, 2007; (B) $6.55 an hour, beginning 12 months after that 60th day; and (C) $7.25 an hour, beginning 24 months after that 60th day; (2) if such employee is a home worker in Puerto Rico or the Virgin Islands, not less than the minimum piece rate prescribed by regulation or order; or, if no such minimum piece rate is in effect, any piece rate adopted by such employer which shall yield, to the proportion or class of employees prescribed by regulation or order, not less than the applicable minimum hourly wage rate. Such minimum piece rates or employer piece rates shall be commensurate with, and shall be paid in lieu of, the minimum hourly wage rate applicable under the provisions of this section. The Administrator, or his authorized representative, shall have power to make such regulations or orders as are necessary or appropriate to carry out any of the provisions of this paragraph, including the power without limiting the generality of the foregoing, to define any operation or occupation which is performed by such home work employees in Puerto Rico or the Virgin Islands; to establish minimum piece rates for any operation or occupation so defined; to prescribe the method and procedure for ascertaining and promulgating minimum piece rates; to prescribe standards for employer piece rates, including the proportion or class of employees who shall
perturbation.justification: The original text states Diplomat Ambassador Eyewear Group "agrees to comply" with laws and regulations. The changed text uses "will endeavor to comply...as they understand them," which weakens the obligation and introduces subjective interpretation. This directly contradicts the FLSA's requirement for strict adherence to minimum wage and overtime rules, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, from 29 U.S.C. §206, states that "Every employer shall pay to each of his employees...wages at the following rates," indicating a mandatory obligation. The changed text allows the company's 'understanding' to dictate compliance, potentially leading to non-compliance with wage laws, creating a legal contradiction as defined by the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Diplomat Ambassador Eyewear Group agrees to comply with any reasonable requests from Kathy Ireland, Inc. concerning labor and safety resolutions.
perturbation.changed_text: Diplomat Ambassador Eyewear Group will consider complying with suggested requests from Kathy Ireland, Inc. regarding labor and safety resolutions.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'agrees to comply' and 'reasonable requests' are changed to 'will consider complying' and 'suggested requests'. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. 'Will consider' implies no guarantee of compliance, and removing 'reasonable' makes any kind of requests uncertain, conflicting with standard labor and safety laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Occupational Safety and Health Act (OSH Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: The OSH Act requires employers to provide a safe and healthful workplace. By making compliance with safety requests optional ('will consider complying'), the modified text contradicts the OSH Act's mandate for employers to actively ensure workplace safety, as it allows for potential disregard of safety requests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'agrees to comply' to 'will consider complying' significantly weakens the obligation related to labor and safety resolutions. The original text creates a clear expectation of compliance, while the modified text introduces ambiguity and discretion. The law explanation emphasizes that OSH Act mandates employers actively ensure workplace safety. By making compliance optional, the changed text contradicts this mandate, as it allows for potential disregard of safety requests, creating a legal contradiction as defined in the 'Ambiguities' perturbation definition.

perturbation.original_text: Diplomat agrees that it will obtain, at its own expense, product liability insurance from a recognized insurance company which is qualified to do business in the State of California providing adequate protection (at least in the amount of $ ) for KI, Inc., KI and Diplomat against any claims, suits, loss or damage arising out of any alleged defects in the products.
perturbation.changed_text: Diplomat will attempt to obtain product liability insurance that they believe provides some protection for KI, Inc., KI and Diplomat against potential claims arising out of defects, subject to availability and cost.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a clear obligation to obtain product liability insurance with 'adequate protection'. The modified text replaces this with a vague intention ('will attempt to obtain') and introduces subjective criteria ('they believe provides some protection'), along with conditions ('subject to availability and cost'). This drastically weakens the obligation and creates ambiguity about the level of protection required, potentially contradicting consumer protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CLRA aims to protect consumers against deceptive practices. By weakening the product liability insurance requirement, the modification increases the risk of consumers being harmed by defective products without adequate recourse. This contradicts the CLRA's intent to ensure consumer protection and remedies for damages caused by faulty goods.
perturbation.location: 8. Indemnification by Licensee and Product Liability Insurance.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 3. Deceptive Practices [1770- 1770.] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1770. (a) The unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices listed in this subdivision undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another. (2) Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person does not have
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates product liability insurance, whereas the changed text only requires Diplomat to 'attempt to obtain' insurance, subject to availability and cost, significantly weakening the obligation. This directly contradicts the intention of consumer protection laws like the CLRA, as indicated by the Law Explanation, which aims to ensure remedies for damages caused by faulty goods. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 1770(a), identifies 'unfair or deceptive acts or practices' in transactions intended to result in the sale of goods or services as unlawful. The weakened insurance obligation increases consumer risk with defective products, suggesting a deceptive practice by reducing recourse. Therefore, a contradiction exists because the modification undermines the spirit and potentially the legal requirements related to consumer protection.
